options.content: A G-protein subunit activates adenylyl cyclase by direct interaction at the membrane.
The receptor’s cytoplasmic domain causes dissociation of G-protein subunits.
The activated G-protein directly hydrolyzes ATP into cyclic AMP and phosphate.
The ligand–receptor complex enters the nucleus to initiate transcription directly.

questions.specification:
![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2Ymexry4TIfE6EQF4dyvi.jpeg)

Why do the ATPases shown require transmembrane regions to function?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B2.1 Membranes and membrane transport > B2.1.7 Pump proteins for active transport
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B2.1.7 Pump proteins for active transport
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Membrane-bound ATPases are pump proteins that use ATP to actively transport ions across the lipid bilayer; transmembrane regions form the pathway through the hydrophobic membrane.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: B - Form and Function > B2.1 Membranes and membrane transport > B2.1.4 Integral and peripheral proteins in membranes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B2.1.4 Integral and peripheral proteins in membranes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks why ATPases require transmembrane regions, relating to integral membrane proteins spanning the bilayer.

options.content: They form a hydrophilic pathway for ions across the hydrophobic bilayer.
options.markscheme: Transmembrane regions allow ion passage through the membrane’s hydrophobic core.

options.content: They bind steroid hormones in the cytoplasm to activate transport.
options.markscheme: Steroid hormone binding is not required for ATPase ion pumping.

options.content: They catalyse ATP synthesis/hydrolysis only in the cytosol.
options.markscheme: Catalysis can occur on one side of the membrane, but transmembrane regions are needed for ion movement across the bilayer.

options.content: They allow water to diffuse freely across the membrane.
options.markscheme: Water diffusion is mainly via aquaporins; ATPases are not required for passive water movement.

questions.specification:
A researcher measured the effect of a ligand binding to a GPCR on cAMP production in liver cells. The data is shown below:

| Ligand Concentration (nM) | cAMP Production (pmol min$^{-1}$) |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 1.0 |
| 5 | 3.8 |
| 10 | 7.5 |
| 25 | 15.2 |
| 50 | 18.5 |
| 100 | 19.0 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C2.1 Chemical signalling (HL) > C2.1.9 Transmembrane receptors that activate G proteins
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C2.1.9 Transmembrane receptors that activate G proteins
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question centers on a ligand binding to a GPCR and downstream second-messenger output (cAMP), i.e., transmembrane receptors that activate G proteins and show saturating response at higher ligand concentrations.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C2.1 Chemical signalling (HL) > C2.1.7 Initiation of signal transduction pathways by receptors
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C2.1.7 Initiation of signal transduction pathways by receptors
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) asks mechanistically why the response plateaus at high ligand concentration, relating to signal transduction pathway initiation and saturation (receptor/effector limitation) after receptor activation.

parts.content: Describe the relationship between ligand concentration and cAMP production using the data provided.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify increasing trend: As ligand concentration increases, cAMP production also increases, indicating activation of the GPCR pathway <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify plateau: Beyond 50 nM, cAMP production plateaus, showing that the pathway reaches a maximum activation level <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the general trend
steps.content:
To describe the relationship, we first look at how cAMP production changes as we add more ligand. Between $0\text{ nM}$ and $50\text{ nM}$, we can see a clear pattern: as the **Ligand Concentration increases**, the **cAMP Production also increases**.

For example, at $5\text{ nM}$, production is $3.8\text{ pmol min}^{-1}$, but it jumps to $15.2\text{ pmol min}^{-1}$ when the concentration reaches $25\text{ nM}$. This indicates a positive correlation, where the ligand is successfully activating the GPCR pathway.

highlights.text: As ligand concentration increases, cAMP production also increases, indicating activation of the GPCR pathway

steps.title: Identify the plateau
steps.content:
Next, we look at what happens at the highest concentrations. Notice the change between $50\text{ nM}$ and $100\text{ nM}$:

*   At $50\text{ nM}$, cAMP production is $18.5\text{ pmol min}^{-1}$.
*   At $100\text{ nM}$, cAMP production is $19.0\text{ pmol min}^{-1}$.

The increase here is very small ($0.5\text{ units}$) compared to the earlier jumps. This shows that the relationship is no longer strictly increasing; instead, it **plateaus beyond 50 nM**. In biology, this usually means the system has reached "saturation," where all available receptors are occupied or the pathway is working at its maximum possible speed.

highlights.text: Beyond 50 nM, cAMP production plateaus, showing that the pathway reaches a maximum activation level

steps.title: Formulate the final description
steps.content:
To earn both marks, combine these two observations into a clear summary:

1.  **Trend:** There is a positive relationship where cAMP production increases as ligand concentration increases from $0$ to $50\text{ nM}$.
2.  **Limit:** The production levels off or plateaus at concentrations above $50\text{ nM}$.

This concept of signal transduction and receptor activation is covered in **Topic C2.1: Chemical Signalling**.

steps.title: Analyze the first segment
steps.content:
To describe the relationship using the **Segmented Data Comparison** approach, we first look at the interval from **0 nM to 50 nM**. 

In this lower concentration range, we see a rapid and significant rise in cAMP production. At $0$ nM, the production is $1.0 \text{ pmol min}^{-1}$, and by $50$ nM, it has climbed to $18.5 \text{ pmol min}^{-1}$. This shows a strong positive correlation where increasing the ligand concentration significantly activates the GPCR pathway.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the initial cAMP value from the value at 50 nM.

calculator.description: Calculate the increase in cAMP production for the first segment (0-50 nM).

steps.title: Analyze the second segment
steps.content:
Next, we examine the interval from **50 nM to 100 nM**. 

Despite adding another $50$ nM of ligand (the same amount added in the first segment), the cAMP production only increases from $18.5$ to $19.0 \text{ pmol min}^{-1}$. This minimal increase of only $0.5 \text{ units}$ indicates that the relationship is no longer linear and the production is reaching a **plateau**. This suggests that the receptors are becoming saturated.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the value at 50 nM from the value at 100 nM.

calculator.description: Calculate the increase in cAMP production for the second segment (50-100 nM).

steps.title: Synthesize the overall relationship
steps.content:
By contrasting these two segments, we can provide a complete description for the marks:

1.  **Positive Trend:** There is an overall increase in cAMP production as ligand concentration increases, which identifies the activation of the GPCR pathway.
2.  **Plateau:** The rate of increase slows down significantly after $50$ nM, and the production plateaus between $50$ and $100$ nM, showing the pathway has reached maximum activation.

This concept of signal transduction and receptor activation is covered in **Topic C2.1: Chemical Signalling**.

highlights.text: Identify increasing trend: As ligand concentration increases, cAMP production also increases, indicating activation of the GPCR pathway
Identify plateau: Beyond 50 nM, cAMP production plateaus, showing that the pathway reaches a maximum activation level

parts.content: Explain why cAMP production reaches a maximum level when ligand concentration exceeds 50 nM.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain receptor saturation: At high ligand concentrations, all available GPCRs become saturated (all receptor binding sites are occupied) <marks>A1</marks>

- Link to maximum activation: No additional receptors are available for ligand binding, so adenylyl cyclase activation reaches its maximum. Therefore, further increases in ligand concentration cannot increase cAMP production <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the data trend
steps.content:
Looking at the table, we can see that as the ligand concentration increases from $0\text{ nM}$ to $50\text{ nM}$, the cAMP production rises sharply. However, between $50\text{ nM}$ and $100\text{ nM}$, the production only increases by a tiny amount ($0.5\text{ pmol min}^{-1}$), indicating that the system has reached a plateau.

In IB Biology, this concept is explored in **Topic C2.1: Chemical Signalling**, where we study how receptors transition from an inactive to an active state upon binding a ligand.

steps.title: Explain receptor saturation
steps.content:
Using the **Receptor Saturation Approach**, we focus on the physical occupancy of the receptors. Every cell has a finite (limited) number of G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) on its membrane. 

When the ligand concentration is very high (exceeding $50\text{ nM}$), every available receptor binding site becomes occupied by a ligand. At this point, the receptors are said to be **saturated**.

steps.title: Link to maximum activation
steps.content:
Since all receptors are already bound to ligands, adding even more ligand molecules has no effect because there are no "empty" receptors left to receive the signal. 

Because the receptors are the "gatekeepers" for the signal, the activation of the downstream enzyme (adenylyl cyclase) reaches its maximum capacity. Therefore, the production of cAMP cannot increase any further, resulting in the maximum level seen in the data.

steps.title: Analyze the data trend
steps.content: First, let's look at the data table. Notice how the cAMP production jumps significantly between $0$ nM and $25$ nM. However, between $50$ nM and $100$ nM, the production only increases from $18.5$ to $19.0$ pmol min$^{-1}$. This indicates that the system is reaching a plateau, or a "maximum velocity."

steps.title: Identify the limiting factor
steps.content:
In this biochemical system, the **G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs)** act as the limiting factor. Just as we see with enzymes in **Topic C1.1 (Enzymes and metabolism)**, a process that relies on a protein binding site will eventually hit a limit when all those sites are busy. 

In this case, the number of receptors on the liver cell membrane is finite. Once the ligand concentration is high enough, every available receptor becomes occupied.

steps.title: Explain receptor saturation
steps.content:
This state is known as **receptor saturation**. When the ligand concentration exceeds $50$ nM, the concentration of the ligand is so high that all GPCR binding sites are constantly filled with ligand molecules. 

According to the markscheme, identifying that all available receptors become **saturated** is the first key point for earning a mark.

highlights.text: At high ligand concentrations, all available GPCRs become saturated (all receptor binding sites are occupied)

steps.title: Link to maximum activation
steps.content:
Since every receptor is already active, the biochemical machinery (G-proteins and the enzyme **adenylyl cyclase**) is already working at its maximum capacity. 

Because there are no "free" receptors left to bind more ligand, increasing the ligand concentration further (e.g., to $100$ nM or $500$ nM) cannot trigger any more adenylyl cyclase. Consequently, the rate of cAMP production cannot increase any further and stays at its maximum level.

highlights.text: No additional receptors are available for ligand binding, so adenylyl cyclase activation reaches its maximum. Therefore, further increases in ligand concentration cannot increase cAMP production

questions.specification:
Researchers investigated the effects of oxytocin (OT) on cognitive recovery after ischemic stroke in a mouse model. The figure below summarizes several interacting molecular pathways influenced by OT, including calpain-1, NF-$\kappa$B, GDNF, VEGF, AQP4, and cytokines such as TGF-$\alpha$ and IL-$1\beta$.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/gkb2rSUZuxe6rsim3HYfM.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C2.1 Chemical signalling (HL) > C2.1.3 Hormones, neurotransmitters, cytokines and calcium ions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C2.1.3 Hormones, neurotransmitters, cytokines and calcium ions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts b–f explicitly contrast hormones, neurotransmitters and cytokines, and ask about calcium ions in signalling; this matches the leaf topic covering hormones, neurotransmitters, cytokines and Ca2+ as signalling molecules.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C2.1 Chemical signalling (HL) > C2.1.5 Localized and distant effects of signalling molecules
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C2.1.5 Localized and distant effects of signalling molecules
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts b and d ask about distant vs local effects of signalling molecules (endocrine vs paracrine/autocrine), directly aligning with localized and distant effects of signalling molecules.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C2.1 Chemical signalling (HL) > C2.1.4 Chemical diversity of hormones and neurotransmitters
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C2.1.4 Chemical diversity of hormones and neurotransmitters
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part e asks why organisms use diverse chemical classes (amines, proteins, peptides, steroids) for signalling, which maps to chemical diversity of hormones and neurotransmitters.

parts.content: Identify which type of signalling molecule oxytocin belongs to and explain why it can exert distant effects after release into the body.
parts.markscheme:
- oxytocin is a (peptide) hormone <marks>1 mark</marks>
- hormones are secreted by endocrine tissue/glands into the bloodstream (circulatory system) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- carried in blood/plasma to target organs/tissues distant from the site of secretion <marks>1 mark</marks>
- only cells with the specific oxytocin receptor respond (target specificity) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why cytokines such as TGF-$\alpha$ and IL-$1\beta$ are classified separately from hormones and neurotransmitters.
parts.markscheme:
- cytokines are signalling proteins/peptides produced by immune cells (and other cells) involved in inflammatory/immune responses <marks>1 mark</marks>
- commonly act locally via paracrine and/or autocrine signalling rather than classic endocrine signalling <marks>1 mark</marks>
- hormones are endocrine signals released into blood to act at a distance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- neurotransmitters are released from neurons and act across a synapse/synaptic cleft on a postsynaptic cell <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the diagram, name one signalling molecule that acts locally (near its release site) and one that acts at distant sites, and justify your choices.
parts.markscheme:
- local example identified: cytokines (TGF-$\alpha$ and/or IL-$1\beta$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- justification: cytokines typically act on nearby cells in the same tissue (paracrine/autocrine) to regulate inflammation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- distant example identified: oxytocin (OT) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- justification: oxytocin is a hormone transported in the bloodstream to act on target tissues away from its release site <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- local example identified: GDNF and/or VEGF <marks>1 mark</marks>
- justification: growth/neurotrophic factors act on nearby neural/vascular cells to promote survival/repair (paracrine) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- distant example identified: oxytocin (OT) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- justification: oxytocin is a hormone transported in the bloodstream to act on target tissues away from its release site <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State one reason why animals use a wide range of chemical substances (such as amines, proteins, peptides, and steroids) for signalling.
parts.markscheme:
- different chemical types have different properties (e.g. solubility, ability to cross membranes, stability/half-life) affecting signalling speed and duration <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- chemical diversity allows specific receptor interactions and fine-tuned regulation of many different physiological processes <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: The signalling pathway shown in the diagram involves the activation of Calpain-1, which is a calcium-dependent enzyme. Describe the role of calcium ions ($Ca^{2+}$) in intracellular signalling and explain their importance in the nervous system.
parts.markscheme:
- $Ca^{2+}$ acts as an intracellular second messenger that changes in concentration to relay/amplify signals inside cells <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $Ca^{2+}$ binds to/activates proteins and enzymes (e.g. calmodulin or other $Ca^{2+}$-dependent enzymes such as calpain) to produce a cellular response <marks>1 mark</marks>
- in neurons, influx of $Ca^{2+}$ at the presynaptic terminal triggers synaptic vesicle fusion/exocytosis and neurotransmitter release <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $Ca^{2+}$ is also important in nervous system processes such as synaptic plasticity and regulation of gene expression in neurons <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline the relationship between a receptor molecule and a specific ligand. Provide an example.
parts.markscheme:
- A receptor molecule is a protein that binds to a specific ligand to form a complex which initiates a biological response <marks>1 mark</marks>
$$ \text{Receptor} + \text{Ligand} \rightleftharpoons \text{Receptor-Ligand Complex} $$

- The binding is usually highly specific, meaning that the receptor will only bind to a particular ligand or a group of closely related ligands <marks>1 mark</marks>

- An example is the binding of insulin (ligand) to the insulin receptor, which triggers cellular uptake of glucose <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
A study measured the glucose uptake rate in muscle cells at different insulin concentrations. The results are shown below:

| Insulin Concentration (µU/mL) | Glucose Uptake Rate (nmol/min) |
|-------------------------------|---------------------------------|
| 0                             | 0.5                             |
| 10                            | 2.3                             |
| 50                            | 4.8                             |
| 100                           | 7.1                             |
| 200                           | 8.5                             |
| 500                           | 8.7                             |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: D - Continuity and Change > D3.3 Homeostasis > D3.3.3 Regulation of blood glucose as an example of the role of hormones in homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D3.3.3 Regulation of blood glucose as an example of the role of hormones in homeostasis
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question examines how insulin concentration affects glucose uptake in muscle cells and asks why the response plateaus, which directly relates to hormonal regulation of blood glucose homeostasis (insulin action and saturation of response).

parts.content: Describe the trend shown in the data.
parts.markscheme:
- As insulin concentration increases, glucose uptake rate increases (positive correlation), showing insulin stimulates glucose uptake.
- The increase is steep at lower insulin concentrations (e.g., 0.5 nmol/min at 0 µU/mL to 7.1 nmol/min at 100 µU/mL).
- Beyond about 200 µU/mL, glucose uptake rate levels off/plateaus at about 8.5–8.7 nmol/min (diminishing returns/approaches a maximum).

<marks>2 marks</marks> : <marks>1 mark</marks> for describing increasing uptake with increasing insulin, <marks>1 mark</marks> for describing the plateau/levelling off beyond about 200 µU/mL

parts.content: Explain why the glucose uptake rate does not increase significantly beyond an insulin concentration of 200 µU/mL.
parts.markscheme:
- At high insulin concentrations, insulin receptors/signalling pathways are saturated/fully activated so additional insulin produces little extra response.
- Maximum translocation/insertion or activation of GLUT4 transporters in the membrane has occurred (finite number available).
- The rate becomes limited by transporter number/turnover (Vmax) or another downstream step, so glucose uptake reaches a maximum and plateaus.

<marks>2 marks</marks> : <marks>1 mark</marks> for linking to saturation/maximum activation of insulin signalling/GLUT4, <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining that a limiting factor/finite number causes a plateau so further insulin has little effect

questions.specification:
The diagram below shows G-protein-coupled receptor (GPCR) signalling. What activates the G-protein in this pathway?

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f697f38e-1e81-48b2-aa5c-734ba75bf9ac)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C2.1 Chemical signalling (HL) > C2.1.9 Transmembrane receptors that activate G proteins

labeledSubSubTopics.title: C2.1.7 Initiation of signal transduction pathways by receptors
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The functional aspect of receptors involves the initiation of signal transduction pathways, which is described in this topic.

parts.content: Outline how receptors function as proteins with binding sites for specific signalling molecules.
parts.markscheme:
- Receptors are proteins with a specific binding site complementary to a signalling molecule (ligand), giving specificity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Binding forms a receptor–ligand complex. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Binding causes a conformational change in the receptor protein. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The conformational change initiates a cellular response (e.g. activation of an intracellular pathway/second messenger, opening of an ion channel). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe the structure of a receptor molecule.
parts.markscheme:
- Many receptors are membrane proteins (glycoproteins) embedded in the plasma membrane. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Extracellular domain with a binding site for the signalling molecule. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transmembrane region (often one or more hydrophobic helices) anchoring the receptor in the membrane. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Intracellular/cytoplasmic domain that interacts with intracellular proteins (e.g. G proteins/kinases) to transmit the signal. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Receptors vary in type (e.g. G-protein-coupled, ion channel-linked, enzyme-linked) but share the feature of ligand binding linked to signal transduction. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how receptor binding leads to signal transduction and a cellular response.
parts.markscheme:
- Ligand binding produces a specific conformational change that activates the receptor. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Activated receptor interacts with intracellular components (e.g. activates a G protein by GDP→GTP exchange / activates an associated kinase). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This leads to production/activation of second messengers (e.g. cAMP, IP$_3$, Ca$^{2+}$) or direct opening/closing of an ion channel. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A signal transduction cascade amplifies the signal (one activated receptor can activate many downstream molecules). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Downstream effectors change cell activity (e.g. altered enzyme activity, changes in membrane permeability, changes in gene expression via transcription factors). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The response is specific to the receptor and cell type due to the intracellular proteins expressed in that cell. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Signal is terminated by ligand dissociation and/or receptor inactivation/internalization and breakdown of second messengers. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline the mechanism of synaptic transmission, including the role of neurotransmitters and receptors.
parts.markscheme:
- Action potential arrives at the synaptic terminal. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Voltage-gated calcium channels open, allowing $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ions to enter the terminal. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The influx of $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ causes synaptic vesicles to fuse with the presynaptic membrane. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Neurotransmitters are released into the synaptic cleft via exocytosis. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Neurotransmitters bind to specific receptors on the postsynaptic membrane. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Binding of neurotransmitters to receptors causes ion channels to open, leading to depolarization or hyperpolarization of the postsynaptic membrane. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The signal is terminated by reuptake of neurotransmitters, enzymatic degradation, or diffusion away from the synaptic cleft. <marks>1 mark</marks>
